text: It's Friday at 4:47 PM. Even your CI pipeline is begging you to stop.
Your on-call engineer just signed off. Who's going to fix this?
You have the confidence of someone who has never been paged at 2 AM.
The sprint ends today. This is desperation, not engineering.
Mercury is in retrograde, but honestly that's not even the biggest risk factor right now.
The last person who deployed on a Friday got a Jira ticket named after them.
It's Tuesday morning. The stars have aligned. Ship it.
Perfect window: the whole team is online and caffeine levels are optimal.
You've done a Thursday deploy before and lived to tell the tale.
The deployment gods smile upon you. For now.
It's Monday. Nobody has broken anything yet. The odds are in your favor.
Deploying during lunch? Bold. Very bold.
Your staging environment says it works. Your staging environment is a liar.
The PR was approved by someone who definitely didn't read it.
You're deploying before your first cup of coffee. Brave.
It's the holiday freeze. Even Santa wouldn't deploy right now.
Friday the 13th. You're not superstitious, but your uptime graph is.
Quarter end. Finance is watching every metric. Choose wisely.
Nobody will believe the incident report if you deploy on April Fools.
Your manager just said 'what could go wrong?' — that's a production curse.
The changelog has more lines than the actual code change. Suspicious.
Your PR has been open for 3 weeks. The codebase has evolved without you.
Three deploys today already. The fourth one is statistically where things break.
The weekend is calling. Your pager is also calling. Coincidence?
You're deploying on a Saturday. Who hurt you?
Mid-morning on a Wednesday. This is the golden hour of deployment.
All tests passing. Code reviewed. Monitoring ready. This is the way.
Your deployment pipeline is green. Suspiciously green.
The sun is shining, the CI is passing, and your feature flag is ready.
Deploy now, before the next standup reveals a blocker.
Your commit message says 'minor fix'. We both know it's not minor.
The database migration looks simple. Famous last words.
You're deploying to production without testing in staging. Respect.
Everyone is in a meeting. Perfect time to deploy... or perfect time for chaos.
Your feature branch was created 47 days ago. It has seen things.
The intern approved the PR. Ship it with confidence (and a rollback plan).
You've got 99 problems but a failed deploy ain't one. Yet.
It's 5:01 PM. Your work-life balance called. It's filing a complaint.
The monitoring dashboard has more red than a Christmas sweater.
Last deploy took 45 minutes to rollback. This one has twice the changes.
Your canary deploy is limping. That's... not what canary means.
The load balancer is sweating and the deploy hasn't even started.
Your team lead is on PTO. Maximum accountability, minimum oversight.
You just said 'it works on my machine.' That's not the flex you think it is.
Tuesday at 10 AM: peak deployment clarity. The engineering gods approve.
Your rollback plan is 'we'll figure it out.' Godspeed.
Deploying with one eye on the clock and one on Slack. What could go wrong?
The sprint retrospective is tomorrow. Deploy now and create new talking points.
Your diff has 3,000 lines. That's not a deploy, that's a rewrite.
You're about to deploy code you wrote at 2 AM. Past-you was not a responsible engineer.
This deploy has been blessed by three senior engineers. That means nothing, but it feels nice.
The feature flag is your parachute. At least you packed one.
Deploying right before a long weekend is a form of self-sabotage.
Your PR description says 'no functional changes.' The git diff disagrees.
You're deploying a 'quick fix' that touches the payment service. Breathe.
The QA team signed off. They also signed off on the last outage.
Early Wednesday morning. Low traffic. Perfect deploy window.
The code freeze starts in an hour. Deploy now or hold your peace.
You haven't deployed in two weeks. The dopamine hit will be worth it.
Your automated tests take 45 minutes. You ran them for 12. Close enough?
The architecture diagram and the actual code parted ways six months ago.
You're deploying because Jira said the ticket was due yesterday.
The error budget is looking healthy. Go ahead and spend some of it.
Your deployment checklist has 47 items. You checked 3.
The CEO is watching the demo tomorrow. No pressure.
Kubernetes says the pods are healthy. Kubernetes also said that right before the last incident.
You're deploying a config change. The simplest changes cause the biggest outages.
Your SRE team has already pre-written the postmortem template. They know.
It's 3 PM on a Thursday. Solid deploy window. Just don't push your luck.
The build succeeded on the first try. That's either great engineering or a broken test suite.
You have a rollback plan, a monitoring dashboard, and anxiety. You're ready.
Deploying with 100% code coverage. The tests don't test anything useful, but the number looks great.
It's 8:57 AM. Three minutes of safety remain.
Your deploy is smaller than your morning coffee order. Ship it.
The incident channel is quiet. Suspiciously quiet.
You've read the diff twice. Understanding it is optional.
Your deploy strategy is 'YOLO'. That's technically a strategy.
The sun will rise tomorrow whether you deploy or not. But will your service?
Deploying after 6 PM means you're either dedicated or delusional.
Your monitoring alerts are set up. They'll tell you about the fire you're about to start.
The team just finished a blameless postmortem. Time to give them material for the next one.
The dependency update 'shouldn't break anything.' Narrator: It broke everything.
You're deploying the same commit that passed in staging. Staging lies, but sometimes it's honest.
It's 10 AM. Optimal focus. Optimal caffeine. Deploy.
The deploy train leaves the station every hour. This one has no brakes.
You merged to main without rebasing. Living dangerously.
Your co-worker just said 'I have a good feeling about this deploy.' Run.
The deploy is small, scoped, and well-tested. Is this real life?
Your service mesh is more tangled than your headphone cables.
Today's deploy is sponsored by stack traces and shattered dreams.
You're deploying on the same day as a company all-hands. Maximum audience for the outage.
The feature has been in development for 6 months. It's not ready, but neither were you at 22.
Wednesday afternoon — a perfectly mediocre time to deploy. We approve.
One does not simply deploy to production. Unless it's Tuesday morning.
Your git log reads like a crime novel. The victim: production.
The chaos monkey is on vacation. Seize the moment.
Your deployment has a bus factor of 1, and that bus is you.
It's 11 AM. Peak deploy energy. Go.
You wrote the code, reviewed the code, and approved the code. Checks and balances!
The only thing stopping you is common sense. And when has that helped?
